front: Police get an anonymous letter predicting a couple's drug-running travel; officers corroborate the innocent details of the prediction and obtain a warrant. The Court tosses the rigid two-prong informant test in favor of a flexible standard. Which case?
back:
**Illinois v. Gates (1983)**
Holding: Probable cause from an informant's tip is judged by the **totality of the circumstances** — the issuing magistrate makes a practical, common-sense decision whether there is a fair probability of crime; an informant's veracity, reliability, and basis of knowledge are relevant factors but not independent, dispositive hurdles.
Signature: "totality-of-the-circumstances" probable cause; corroboration of predicted future conduct can carry a deficient tip.
Why it matters: abandoned the rigid Aguilar-Spinelli two-prong test; the controlling federal standard for probable cause from tips.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Aguilar / Spinelli** (the abandoned rigid two-prong: basis of knowledge AND veracity each required) — Gates folds them into one flexible balance.

front: A warrant affidavit recites only that officers 'have received reliable information from a credible person' that narcotics are at the home — no facts about how the informant knew or why he's believable. Which case set the (now-defunct) test that struck this down?
back:
**Aguilar v. Texas (1964)**
Holding: A magistrate may issue a warrant on an informant's hearsay only if the affidavit shows **(1) the informant's basis of knowledge** (how he got the information) **and (2) his veracity/reliability** (why he should be believed).
Signature: the two-prong informant test — "basis of knowledge" + "veracity."
Why it matters: with Spinelli, the rigid pre-Gates framework for evaluating informant tips.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Gates** (1983), which **replaced** this rigid two-prong with a totality-of-the-circumstances balance.

front: An FBI affidavit pairs an informant's bare tip that a man is running a phone-based bookmaking operation with surveillance showing he visits an apartment with two phones. The Court refines the two-prong informant test — and finds the tip still falls short. Which case?
back:
**Spinelli v. United States (1969)**
Holding: Refined Aguilar's two-prong test — a tip that fails the prongs can be salvaged only if independent corroboration is detailed enough to show the informant gained his information in a reliable way; the bare tip plus innocent corroboration here was insufficient.
Signature: the elaborated Aguilar-Spinelli two-prong analysis.
Why it matters: marked the high-water mark of the rigid two-prong approach — expressly **abandoned by Illinois v. Gates** in favor of totality of the circumstances.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Gates** (totality test that overruled this approach) or **Draper** (where the corroborated tip DID establish probable cause).

front: An informant tells an agent that a man will arrive on a specific train from Chicago wearing particular clothes and walking fast; the man steps off exactly as described. The Court holds the verified details established probable cause to arrest. Which case?
back:
**Draper v. United States (1959)**
Holding: When police independently corroborate the predictive details of an informant's tip — right down to the suspect's clothing and arrival — the verified accuracy gives probable cause that the remaining, unverified assertion (he's carrying drugs) is also true.
Signature: corroborated informant tip = probable cause; the model the later tip cases build on.
Why it matters: the foundational corroboration case; Gates relied on its logic that confirming predicted details vouches for the tip as a whole.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Spinelli** (corroboration of merely innocent, non-predictive detail there was NOT enough) — Draper's corroboration was of specific predicted future conduct.

front: A defendant claims the affidavit supporting his search warrant contained deliberate lies. Which case entitles him to a hearing to challenge the truthfulness of a facially valid warrant affidavit?
back:
**Franks v. Delaware (1978)**
Holding: A defendant is entitled to an evidentiary hearing on a warrant affidavit if he makes a **substantial preliminary showing** that the affiant included a false statement **knowingly and intentionally, or with reckless disregard for the truth**, and the falsehood was necessary to the probable-cause finding; if proven and the remaining content is insufficient, the warrant is voided and the fruits suppressed.
Signature: the "Franks hearing" for deliberate or reckless falsehoods in a warrant affidavit.
Why it matters: the mechanism for attacking the veracity (not just the facial sufficiency) of a warrant; a knowing or reckless falsehood is also the first of Leon's four exceptions to the good-faith rule (good-faith reliance won't save such a warrant).
⚠ Mere negligence or innocent mistake in the affidavit does NOT trigger a Franks hearing — the false statement must be intentional or reckless.

front: A state Attorney General — who was also the chief investigator and prosecutor in the case — personally signs the search warrant for the suspect's car. The Court holds the warrant invalid because of who issued it. Which case?
back:
**Coolidge v. New Hampshire (1971)**
Holding: A warrant must be issued by a **neutral and detached magistrate**; a warrant signed by the state Attorney General (who was leading the prosecution) is void because he was not neutral — a per se rule of disqualification, so the search stands as if there were no warrant at all.
Signature: "neutral and detached magistrate" — the issuer cannot be the prosecutor/investigator.
Why it matters: anchors the neutral-magistrate requirement of the Warrant Clause (also the plain-view doctrine's origin case).
⚠ Don't confuse with **Lo-Ji Sales** (a magistrate who started neutral but ABANDONED that role by joining the search party) — Coolidge's issuer was disqualified from the outset.

front: A town justice signs an open-ended warrant to seize 'obscene' items at an adult bookstore, then personally rides along with the police, browses the store, and decides item-by-item what to seize. The Court invalidates it. Which case?
back:
**Lo-Ji Sales, Inc. v. New York (1979)**
Holding: A magistrate forfeits his neutral-and-detached character when he joins and effectively leads the search party and makes on-the-scene seizure decisions; the open-ended warrant (filled in during the search) was an unconstitutional general warrant lacking particularity.
Signature: the magistrate who became "an adjunct law enforcement officer."
Why it matters: shows the neutral-magistrate requirement is about conduct, not just title; one of Leon's named exceptions where good faith won't save a warrant.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Coolidge** (issuer disqualified from the start — the prosecutor) — here a proper magistrate abandoned neutrality during execution.
cite: Lo-Ji Sales, Inc. v. New York, 442 U.S. 319 (1979)

front: An ATF agent's warrant describes the place to be searched but, in the box for the items to be seized, mistakenly describes the house itself — listing no weapons or contraband at all. The Court holds the warrant facially void. Which case?
back:
**Groh v. Ramirez (2004)**
Holding: A warrant that utterly **fails to describe the persons or things to be seized** is facially invalid under the Particularity Clause — even if the supporting affidavit is particular — and the search is presumptively unreasonable; the officer who drafted and led it was not entitled to qualified immunity because no reasonable officer could believe such a warrant valid.
Signature: particularity is non-delegable — the **warrant itself** must describe what may be seized; "so facially deficient" no good-faith reliance is reasonable.
Why it matters: the modern Particularity Clause anchor; ties to Leon's fourth good-faith exception (facially deficient warrants).
⚠ Don't confuse with **Maryland v. Garrison** (a reasonable, honest mistake about the premises was upheld) — Groh's defect was a facial failure of particularity, not a good-faith error.

front: Officers get a warrant for 'the third-floor apartment,' reasonably believing there's only one; in fact there are two, and they begin searching the wrong tenant's unit before realizing the mistake. The Court upholds the search. Which case?
back:
**Maryland v. Garrison (1987)**
Holding: A warrant's validity is judged on the information reasonably available to officers when they sought it; a reasonable, good-faith mistake about the premises (here, that one apartment occupied the whole floor) does not invalidate the warrant or the search conducted before the error became apparent.
Signature: the **reasonable-mistake** rule for warrant execution — "sufficient particularity" judged by what was reasonably knowable.
Why it matters: tempers particularity with objective reasonableness; officers must stop once they realize they're in the wrong place.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Groh** (a facial particularity failure — NOT excused) — Garrison was an honest, reasonable mistake about a particular-on-its-face warrant.

front: A warrant is issued to search a home for child pornography, but it won't take effect unless and until a controlled package is actually delivered and taken inside. Which case upheld this kind of warrant?
back:
**United States v. Grubbs (2006)**
Holding: **Anticipatory warrants** — warrants that take effect only upon a future 'triggering condition' — are not categorically unconstitutional; they require the magistrate to find it now probable that (1) the triggering condition will occur and (2) if it does, contraband will be at the place to be searched.
Signature: anticipatory warrants are valid; "no different in principle from ordinary warrants."
Why it matters: confirms probable cause can rest on conditions to be satisfied in the future, so long as both probabilities are established up front.
⚠ The Fourth Amendment does not require the triggering condition itself to be spelled out on the face of the warrant.

front: Which case held that the common-law requirement that police knock and announce their presence before entering a home is part of the Fourth Amendment reasonableness inquiry?
back:
**Wilson v. Arkansas (1995)**
Holding: The common-law **knock-and-announce** principle — that officers must announce their presence and authority before forcibly entering a dwelling — is an element of the Fourth Amendment reasonableness analysis; but it is not a rigid rule and yields to countervailing law-enforcement interests (threat of violence, destruction of evidence, escape).
Signature: knock-and-announce is constitutionally embedded in 'reasonableness.'
Why it matters: constitutionalized the knock-and-announce rule and framed it as flexible, setting up the exceptions.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Richards** (held there's no blanket exception for felony drug cases — it's case-by-case) or **Hudson** (the violation does NOT trigger the exclusionary rule — remedy bucket).

front: A state court tried to create a blanket rule that police never have to knock and announce when executing a warrant in a felony drug investigation. The Supreme Court rejected the categorical exception. Which case?
back:
**Richards v. Wisconsin (1997)**
Holding: There is **no blanket exception** to knock-and-announce for entire categories of crime (e.g., felony drug cases); the no-knock decision must be evaluated case-by-case — officers need **reasonable suspicion** that knocking would be dangerous, futile, or would allow destruction of evidence.
Signature: rejected a per se drug-case exception; established the 'reasonable suspicion' standard for a no-knock entry.
Why it matters: defines the threshold for dispensing with knock-and-announce — reasonable suspicion, made individually.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Wilson** (which first put knock-and-announce in the Fourth Amendment) — Richards sets the standard for excusing it.

front: Officers with a drug warrant knock, shout 'police search warrant,' wait 15 to 20 seconds with no answer, then break the door with a battering ram — catching the suspect in the shower. The Court holds the short wait reasonable. Which case?
back:
**United States v. Banks (2003)**
Holding: A **15-to-20-second wait** after knocking and announcing, in a drug case, was reasonable before forced entry — the relevant clock is the time it would take to destroy the evidence (flush the cocaine), not the time to reach the front door; reasonableness is judged on the totality of the circumstances.
Signature: 15-20 seconds = reasonable wait when exigency (evidence destruction) is in play.
Why it matters: gives a concrete benchmark for how long police must wait before forcing entry on a knock-and-announce warrant.
⚠ The reasonable wait turns on the exigency (imminent disposal of drugs), not on whether the occupant actually heard or had time to answer.

front: As officers arrive to execute a search warrant on a house, they detain the resident who is leaving and hold him while they search. The Court holds they may detain occupants during the search. Which case?
back:
**Michigan v. Summers (1981)**
Holding: A warrant to search a residence for contraband carries the limited authority to **detain the occupants of the premises** while the search is conducted — a detention justified by officer safety, orderly completion of the search, and preventing flight, and supported by the warrant itself (no separate probable cause needed).
Signature: occupants may be detained incident to executing a search warrant.
Why it matters: the foundational rule allowing detention during warrant execution — later cabined geographically by Bailey.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Bailey** (the detention power is limited to occupants in the **immediate vicinity** of the premises) or **Ybarra** (a search warrant for a place does NOT authorize searching bystanders).

front: Officers with a search warrant watch a man leave the house, follow him, and stop him about a mile away, then detain him under the authority to hold occupants during a search. The Court holds the detention unlawful. Which case?
back:
**Bailey v. United States (2013)**
Holding: The Summers rule authorizing detention of occupants during a search-warrant execution is **limited to the immediate vicinity of the premises**; once a person has left and is no longer in the immediate vicinity, his detention must be justified on other grounds (e.g., Terry reasonable suspicion or probable cause).
Signature: the spatial limit on Summers — detention only in the 'immediate vicinity.'
Why it matters: caps Summers geographically so police can't follow and seize departed occupants on the warrant alone.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Michigan v. Summers** (which authorizes the detention in the first place — at or near the scene) — Bailey is the limiting case.

front: Police have a warrant to search a tavern and its bartender for heroin; while there, they pat down and search a customer who simply happened to be drinking at the bar. The Court suppresses what they found on him. Which case?
back:
**Ybarra v. Illinois (1979)**
Holding: A warrant to search a place (and a named person) does **not** authorize searching other people who happen to be present; a search of a person requires probable cause **particularized with respect to that person** — mere presence at a lawfully searched premises is not enough.
Signature: "probable cause, particularized with respect to that person" — no searching the patrons.
Why it matters: the limit on premises warrants — they don't sweep in bystanders; each person searched needs individualized justification.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Michigan v. Summers** (occupants of a home may be **detained** during a search) — Ybarra bars **searching** mere bystanders absent individualized probable cause.
